ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning air and winds.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Air is the most subtle element, and is moist and warm: heavier, indeed, than fire: but lighter than earth and water: it is the cause of respiration and voice: it is colourless, that is, it has no colour by nature: it is clear and transparent, for it is capable of receiving light: it ministers to three of our senses, for it is by its aid that we see, hear and smell: it has the power likewise of receiving heat and cold, dryness and moisture, and its movements in space are up, down, within, without, to the right and to the left, and the cyclical movement.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It does not derive its light from itself, but is illuminated by sun, and moon, and stars, and fire.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And this is just what the Scripture means when it says, And darkness was upon the deep; for its object is to shew that the air has not derived its light from itself, but that it is quite a different essence from light.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And wind is a movement of air: or wind is a rush of air which changes its name as it changes the place whence it rushes.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Its place is in the air.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For place is the circumference of a body.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But what is it that surrounds bodies but air?

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: There are, moreover, different places in which the movement of air originates, and from these the winds get their names.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: There are in all twelve winds.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It is said that air is just fire after it has been extinguished, or the vapour of heated water.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: At all events, in its own special nature the air is warm, but it becomes cold owing to the proximity of water and earth, so that the lower parts of it are cold, and the higher warm.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: These then are the winds: Cæcias, or Meses, arises in the region where the sun rises in summer.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Subsolanus, where the sun rises at the equinoxes.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Eurus, where it rises in winter.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Africus, where it sets in winter.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Favonius, where it sets at the equinoxes, and Corus, or Olympias, or Iapyx, where it sets in summer.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Then come Auster and Aquilo, whose blasts oppose one another.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Between Aquilo and Cæcias comes Boreas: and between Eurus and Auster, Phœnix or Euronotus; between Auster and Africus, Libonotus or Leuconotus: and lastly, between Aquilo and Corus, Thrascias, or Cercius, as it is called by the inhabitants of that region.
